jokes.text: Drake's 'Started From the Bottom' plays as Abbi attempts to deposit her $8,000 check at the bank — a grandiose anthem for a modest windfall
jokes.setup: Drake's anthem about rising from poverty plays with full earnestness over the opening sequence
jokes.punchline: The 'bottom' is a single illustration sold for $8K — genuinely exciting to Abbi but a comic mismatch for the song's epic scale
jokes.explanation: The joke is structural and contextual — Drake's song is about generational poverty and superstardom; here it soundtracks a freelance illustrator depositing $8K. The gap between the anthem's grandiosity and the actual stakes is the joke. Works well as an opener, economical, character-specific to broke-but-aspirational Abbi.

jokes.text: Ilana hype-commentates to the bank teller about Abbi's illustration sale: 'This bitch right here drew this illustration, and a sexy-ass new dating web site bought it for $8,000!'
jokes.setup: A bank transaction — a formal, private financial moment
jokes.punchline: Ilana treats it like a rap hype-man performance, offering unsolicited biography to a stranger in a professional setting
jokes.explanation: Deeply Ilana — the hyper-loyal, boundary-free best friend who makes every moment a celebration regardless of context. 'Sexy-ass new dating web site' is also an ironic plant given what that web site turns out to be. The social inappropriateness is funny in itself; the specificity of phrasing ('this bitch right here') makes it quotable.

jokes.text: 'Hey, came from her heart.' — Ilana doubles down to the teller as if that's relevant banking information
jokes.setup: The teller is trying to process a routine deposit
jokes.punchline: Ilana inserts emotional context about artistic authenticity into a financial transaction
jokes.explanation: A quick button on joke 3 — less a standalone joke than a coda, but the economy is excellent. 'Came from her heart' as banking disclosure is absurd and character-true. Brevity saves it.

jokes.text: Ilana sings a spontaneous Ludacris-style jingle at the teller: '♪ For somebody who works at the bank, I thought you'd be more responsible with money ♪' — then just says 'Ludacris.'
jokes.setup: The teller asks how Abbi wants the money — a routine question
jokes.punchline: Ilana breaks into original rap-jingle berating the bank teller for financial irresponsibility, then deadpan-credits the style to Ludacris
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is perfect: a mundane banking exchange → Ilana performs an original song berating the teller → then attributes it to Ludacris as if that's normal citation practice. The one-word button 'Ludacris.' is the ideal economy move. Deeply character-specific to Ilana's anarchic, hype-energy social style. The song lyric itself is logically absurd (why would a bank teller be responsible with money?) in a way that sounds almost reasonable, which is the sweet spot of Broad City absurdism.

jokes.text: Ilana at the bank: 'I also have some business with the bank. I'd like to cash these nickels, and I'll have them in quarters, please.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been acting as Abbi's hype-woman; we assume she's just there for support
jokes.punchline: She has her own 'bank business' — a bag of nickels she wants converted to quarters, delivered with the gravity of a hedge fund manager
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Abbi's $8K and Ilana's nickel-to-quarter exchange is the joke — both treated with equal seriousness. Also plants the nickel detail that pays off late in the episode at the cable office. 'I'll have them in quarters, please' as if it's a special request is perfect economy.

jokes.text: Abbi comes home to find Bevers 'straightening up the living room' — holding a blanket, surrounded by Abbi's belongings arranged as masturbation props
jokes.setup: Bevers claims to be 'just straightening up in the living room'
jokes.punchline: The visual evidence — Body Butter, lap pillow, and Julianna Margulies on TV — tells a different story before Abbi even says it
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom structure (walking in on someone) executed with Broad City specificity — the catalog of items (Body Butter! Lap pillow! Julianna Margulies!) is delivered as rapid-fire accusatory inventory, which escalates the reveal. The visual gag of Bevers 'straightening up' is well-staged.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'You were jerking off. In the communal space! You don't even live here!'
jokes.setup: Abbi catches Bevers masturbating in her apartment
jokes.punchline: Her grievances escalate in the wrong order — the privacy violation lands first, but 'you don't even live here' is the real kicker that reframes the entire relationship
jokes.explanation: The three-beat escalation works: communal space (already bad) → you don't even live here (worse). Short, punchy, character-true for the perpetually exasperated Abbi.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'I wasn't even touching my wiener yet. I was still just doing butt stuff.'
jokes.setup: Abbi accuses Bevers of jerking off
jokes.punchline: Bevers clarifies he hadn't gotten to the main event yet — 'butt stuff' is framed as an innocent warm-up, not the crime
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The structure is a denial that makes everything worse — Bevers offers 'I wasn't touching my wiener yet' as a defense, which is not a defense. 'Still just doing butt stuff' reframes solo butt activity as a preliminary, innocent phase, which is absurd and deeply uncomfortable. 'Wiener' is perfectly Bevers — juvenile vocabulary for adult behavior. Highly quotable, character-specific, economical. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Bevers, I swear to God that better be E.R.' / Bevers: 'It's... Good Wife.'
jokes.setup: Abbi, furious about catching Bevers masturbating, makes one final demand about which Julianna Margulies show he was using
jokes.punchline: She can almost forgive ER — a prestige classic — but Good Wife is somehow worse, a more calculated, less defensible choice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant little joke. The comedic logic is impeccable: Abbi having a hierarchy of acceptable masturbation-to-Margulies content is already funny. 'Good Wife' being the wrong answer — implying Abbi would have been less upset about ER — is unexpected and reveals a weirdly specific internal code of honor. Bevers's guilty, defeated 'It's... Good Wife' with the pause is perfectly structured. One of the episode's highlights.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'You have a way of tainting everything I love.' / Bevers: 'Taint? Don't.'
jokes.setup: Abbi expresses existential frustration about Bevers corrupting her life
jokes.punchline: Bevers intercepts 'tainting' as a reference to the anatomical taint, stopping her mid-lament — especially resonant given he was just doing 'butt stuff'
jokes.explanation: The pun is earned by context — 'taint' lands especially hard because we've just heard about butt stuff. The callback makes it a rewatch bonus. 'Don't.' is the perfect two-word punchline: Bevers is actually self-aware enough to flag it but not self-aware enough to be embarrassed. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: Abbi declares she's moving out — immediately, emphatically — with the energy of someone who's been wronged on a historical scale
jokes.setup: A man was masturbating in her living room
jokes.punchline: Abbi's response — 'I'm a boss. I'm a badass boss!' — is self-hype delivered at a volume and intensity that is wildly disproportionate to the $8K she just deposited
jokes.explanation: Callbacks to the Drake opener and the $8K — 'I got money now' echoes the bank scene's grandiosity. The joke is Abbi's self-conception exceeding her actual circumstances. Functional character comedy but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Bevers's reaction shot — 'Yeah, see how it goes. You see how it goes!' — he doesn't believe she'll actually move
jokes.setup: Abbi declares she's moving out forever
jokes.punchline: Bevers, unfazed, predicts she won't — the 'see how it goes' implying this has happened before and he's always right
jokes.explanation: Bevers's comfortable, dismissive confidence is funnier than anything he could say — the implication that this is routine. Works as a character beat more than a joke.

jokes.text: Bevers quietly says 'Sorry' — a single syllable, totally unconvincing, as Abbi leaves
jokes.setup: Abbi storms out after the masturbation confrontation
jokes.punchline: Bevers's one-word apology — clearly pro forma, delivered with zero urgency — undercuts the drama
jokes.explanation: Quiet button on the Bevers sequence. The understated apology after that scene is the right comic choice — he's not even sorry enough to be emphatic about it.

jokes.text: Ilana's mantra for frustration: 'Human being! Huge feelings.' — shouted into the air
jokes.setup: Ilana is frustrated about a cable billing dispute
jokes.punchline: She processes emotion by narrating her own humanity at full volume in public — as if reminding bystanders and the universe that she exists and has interior states
jokes.explanation: One of the most Ilana lines in the episode. 'Human being! Huge feelings.' is a four-word philosophy — the kind of thing only this character would shout in public and mean sincerely. It's absurdist self-narration that reveals Ilana's worldview: life is dramatic, she is at its center, and feelings require public acknowledgment. Extremely quotable, extremely economical. High character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana confirms she did return the cable box, then reminds Abbi: 'That old lady spit on you in line.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks if they returned the cable box together
jokes.punchline: The detail Ilana volunteers to confirm the memory — an old lady spitting on Abbi — is delivered as a casual landmark, not a traumatic event
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the nonchalance — being spit on by a stranger is memorable only because it happened, not because it's unusual in their world. New York horror delivered as prosaic memory trigger. Good economy.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana see their real estate broker Pam for the first time — a long beat of stunned silence and stuttered 'Hi's
jokes.setup: They've been expecting a normal broker; 'Hi, girls. It's me, Pam.' introduces someone whose appearance clearly shocks them
jokes.punchline: The extended 'Oh— Oh...' and halting 'Hi. Hi.' from both women suggests Pam is spectacularly odd-looking or behaving in an unexpected way — the joke is entirely in the reaction and duration of the beat
jokes.explanation: Classic visual-comedy beat that the transcript can only partially capture. The extended awkward greeting — 'Oh— Oh...' then 'Hi. Hi.' — tells us everything about Pam before she speaks again. In context, Pam is clearly a comedic creation (her subsequent dialogue confirms this). Good silent-comedy structure.

jokes.text: Pam, just before showing apartments: 'Don't shake my hand, it was just down my pants.'
jokes.setup: Introductions are being made; Pam extends common social protocol
jokes.punchline: She preemptively warns against handshaking because her hand was in her pants — stated with professional neutrality, as if this is a normal disclosure
jokes.explanation: Excellent character introduction line. The inversion — being warned NOT to shake hands — is funnier than any amount of explanation. 'It was just down my pants' delivered without shame or apology establishes Pam as someone who exists outside normal social awareness. Economy is near-perfect. Would fit the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Pam shows a literal hallway as an apartment: 'It is a beautiful railroad-style apartment in your budget.'
jokes.setup: They've been taken to view an apartment
jokes.punchline: It's a hallway — Pam rebrands it as 'railroad-style' with real estate language, which is both accurate and completely insane
jokes.explanation: NYC apartment-hunting absurdism — the 'railroad-style' spin is a real estate euphemism taken to its logical extreme. Works as observational comedy about the NYC rental market and Pam's delusional salesmanship.

jokes.text: Pam: 'Where isn't the bathroom?' — in response to being asked where the bathroom is in the hallway apartment
jokes.setup: Abbi asks a reasonable question about bathroom location in an apartment that is a hallway
jokes.punchline: Pam's Zen non-answer implies you can relieve yourself anywhere — framed philosophically, executed as a real estate sales technique
jokes.explanation: The non-answer as answer is a classic comedic move, but 'Where isn't the bathroom?' is so perfectly unhinged it elevates the form. Pam's character is defined by a complete disconnect from normal client-service dynamics. Highly economical.

jokes.text: Ilana's response: 'Jeez, wear a catheter, go in the corner.'
jokes.setup: Pam implies the bathroom is wherever you want it to be
jokes.punchline: Ilana riffs on the implication with a practical, deranged suggestion — catheter + corner = solved
jokes.explanation: Ilana's willingness to run with absurd logic is her core comic trait. The catheter suggestion is a yes-and that outdoes the original absurdity. Good but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Pam: 'You can fit a king size bed in here.' / Ilana: 'Yeah, if you fold it up like a taco.'
jokes.setup: Pam pitches the hallway's spatial potential
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'fold it up like a taco' is a specific, vivid, and logically impossible image that skewers the real estate sales pitch perfectly
jokes.explanation: The taco simile is unexpectedly specific and visceral — 'fold it up like a taco' conveys both the spatial impossibility and Ilana's food-brained imagery perfectly. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'God, why am I so turned on right now?' — after the taco bed comment
jokes.setup: They're standing in a depressing hallway being sold as an apartment
jokes.punchline: Ilana is inexplicably aroused — the specificity of 'right now' implies this is a surprise to her too
jokes.explanation: Deeply Ilana — her sexuality is a constant background hum that surfaces at inappropriate moments. The question form ('why am I') suggests she's genuinely curious about her own arousal, which is funnier than a declarative statement would be.

jokes.text: Pam: 'Don't bully me, all right — I can't take it! I was cyberbullied within an inch of my life last night.'
jokes.setup: Abbi dismisses the hallway apartment and tells Pam to move on
jokes.punchline: Pam takes mild professional rejection as bullying, then reveals she was catastrophically cyberbullied the previous night — the word 'cyberbullied' and 'within an inch of my life' are a catastrophic tonal mismatch
jokes.explanation: 'Within an inch of my life' is a phrase for physical violence — applied to cyberbullying, it's a perfect overstatement. Pam's inability to maintain professional distance while hauling her personal trauma into apartment viewings is funny and sad. Character-specific: Pam is a chaos agent who cannot self-regulate.

jokes.text: Pam: 'I make dolls out of human hair. Who's thirsty?'
jokes.setup: Pam has just disclosed her cyberbullying trauma
jokes.punchline: She follows up with a completely unconnected admission about making human-hair dolls, then immediately pivots to hospitality with 'Who's thirsty?' — three non-sequiturs in a row
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. Three-part structure: cyberbullying disclosure → human hair doll confession → 'Who's thirsty?' The human hair dolls are disturbing enough on their own; the instant pivot to cheerful hostess mode is the killer move. 'Who's thirsty?' has the rhythm of a hostess at a children's birthday party. Pam is revealed as someone who compartmentalizes by never compartmentalizing. Economy is extraordinary — nine words, three distinct comedic beats. Strong 8+ territory.

jokes.text: Pam, in the car: 'God, I hope there's not another speed bump. I've got 18 stitches down there in my Susie.'
jokes.setup: They're driving to the next apartment
jokes.punchline: Pam reveals she has 18 vaginal stitches, names the area 'my Susie,' and is worried about bumpy roads — all in one sentence, unprompted
jokes.explanation: The overshare escalates: 18 stitches is specific (not just 'stitches'), 'down there' briefly teases that she might be circumspect, and then 'my Susie' fully reverses that. The car journey framing makes it more intimate and inescapable. Pam's endearingly bespoke naming conventions for her anatomy are a character-specific comedic device.

jokes.text: Pam: 'You know, the doctor said they'd disintegrate, but they haven't. Maybe I should get 'em yanked out. Maybe I can yank 'em out. You got any tweezers on ya?'
jokes.setup: Pam has just disclosed the 18 vaginal stitches
jokes.punchline: She escalates from medical update → self-surgery consideration → soliciting tweezers from clients in the car — each step worse than the last
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation structure — each beat is worse than the prior one. The tweezer request is the perfect endpoint because it involves the clients in a deeply unwanted way. The transition from medical concern to cheerful DIY is Pam's signature move.

jokes.text: Pam: 'God, I love this car. It's so little, you know, you can basically park it anywhere.' — while Ilana is on hold with the cable company
jokes.setup: Moments after discussing self-surgery with tweezers, Pam pivots to car appreciation
jokes.punchline: The mundane car enthusiasm is darkly funny after the stitches conversation — Pam's emotional range swings from medical overshare to car-owner contentment with no transition
jokes.explanation: Works as a Pam character beat — the tonal whiplash is the joke. Less funny in isolation, more funny in context of Pam's chaotic personality pattern.

jokes.text: The cable company hold recording: 'Thank you for holding. You are no longer on pre-hold. You are on actual hold.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been on hold with the cable company
jokes.punchline: The hold system has 'pre-hold' as a distinct category — the bureaucratic non-progress of being promoted from pre-hold to actual hold is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Pre-hold' is an inspired invention — it perfectly satirizes corporate customer service Kafkaism. The 'promotion' to 'actual hold' is an achievement that means nothing. Then Ilana is immediately placed back on pre-hold. The structure is a three-beat escalation of futility. Observational comedy elevated by strong conceptual specificity.

jokes.text: Pam: 'It's a prewar building built right after the Iraq war.'
jokes.setup: Pam describes the next apartment's historical credentials
jokes.punchline: 'Prewar' is a real estate term meaning pre-WWII (pre-1940s); Pam applies it to the Iraq War (2003), making the building roughly 20 years old and the phrase meaningless
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. It requires knowledge of what 'prewar' means in NYC real estate — and then reveals Pam either doesn't know or doesn't care. The logic is internally consistent (it IS post-Iraq war, technically) which makes it more insidious than a simple non-sequitur. Character-perfect: Pam using the right vocabulary with completely wrong content. One of the episode's best written lines.

jokes.text: Pam: 'How's that mattress treatin' ya?' / 'I can't sleep like this. I feel like I'm in a coffin.' / Pam: 'I slept in a coffin once. It wasn't that bad, except for the body.'
jokes.setup: The current tenant complains that sleeping on a floor mattress feels like being in a coffin
jokes.punchline: Pam confirms she has slept in an actual coffin and reports it 'wasn't that bad' — except for the pre-existing body in it
jokes.explanation: Structure is perfect: the tenant's 'coffin' is metaphorical → Pam takes it literally and reports back from experience → 'except for the body' is the button that transforms a comforting anecdote into horror. 'Except for the body' implies the coffin was occupied at the time, which raises questions the show has no interest in answering. Maximum economy (five words to convey a story we never get to hear). Pam-defining line. Comparable to an 8+ anchor.

jokes.text: Ilana and Abbi scramble out of Pam's tiny car — physical comedy of getting untangled, 'Your hand's on my ass'
jokes.setup: They've pulled up to the next apartment location and need to exit the small car quickly
jokes.punchline: Exiting the tiny car is a physical ordeal involving accidental ass-contact and a ladder
jokes.explanation: Visual/physical comedy that the transcript undersells. Callback to Pam's 'it's so little' car comment. Standard physical comedy beat — funny in performance, less interesting on the page.

jokes.text: Ilana yells at the cable company hold robot: '(Bleep) you, robot!'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been cycling through pre-hold and actual hold
jokes.punchline: Her rage boils over into screaming at an automated system — the insult 'robot' is the best she can do and she commits fully
jokes.explanation: Relatable frustration, but the joke is mostly in the delivery rather than the writing. '(Bleep) you, robot' is too generic to be exceptional.

jokes.text: Pam: 'Come on, let's hurry up. I gotta get home and feed my Oscar fish.'
jokes.setup: Urgency is needed to see the next apartment
jokes.punchline: Pam's urgency isn't professional — it's that she needs to feed a fish named Oscar. Her priorities are completely inverted from what a broker's should be.
jokes.explanation: Pam's pet name adds specificity — it's not just 'my fish,' it's Oscar. The real estate professional who rushes showings for her Oscar fish is a clean character detail.

jokes.text: Pam: 'Hey, you guys know that television show Friends? Yes, it's a great show, right? Here's this place.'
jokes.setup: Pam appears to be setting up an observation about Friends — perhaps a relevant comparison
jokes.punchline: She simply affirms that Friends is great and immediately moves on — the Friends reference has no point and no punchline; it's a non-joke that exposes Pam's complete incompetence as a connector of ideas
jokes.explanation: A meta-structural joke: the setup is a setup, but the punchline is that there's no punchline. Pam builds anticipation of a Friends-apartment comparison (reasonable in context) and then just... moves on. 'Here's this place' is the non-punchline that IS the punchline. The humor is in the deflation. This is harder to write than it looks — you have to commit to the absence of payoff. Strong 8 territory.

jokes.text: Behind the apartment wall: a muffled voice screaming 'Help! Please call the police! They tied me up! I think they're Filipino! They took my baby!'
jokes.setup: Pam is pitching the apartment; the walls are 'super thick'
jokes.punchline: A person is audibly pleading for rescue from what sounds like a kidnapping — including the specific detail 'I think they're Filipino' — and it's treated as a side feature rather than a crisis
jokes.explanation: Structurally brilliant — Pam has JUST said 'the walls are super thick' as a selling point, and then we hear a kidnapping through them. The pitch becomes its own undoing. 'I think they're Filipino' is a perfect absurdist detail: the captive, in crisis, is being weirdly specific and possibly wrong about their captors' ethnicity, which humanizes the chaos. 'They took my baby!' escalates it further. The fact that no one calls the police is the darkest comedic beat.

jokes.text: Pam, after Ilana leaves the apartment: '...The walls are really thick.'
jokes.setup: The screaming kidnapping victim just demonstrated that the walls are NOT thick
jokes.punchline: Pam's sales pitch continues, unmoved, as if the proof-of-concept just ran in the opposite direction
jokes.explanation: Exceptional callback. The joke is entirely structural: Pam introduced 'thick walls' as a feature → the walls proved extremely thin via kidnapping screams → Pam returns to the same claim after Ilana has left. Five words of perfect economy. The fact that she says it to no one, to the empty room, makes it even funnier — Pam is a saleswoman in an empty theater. Economy is as close to 10 as it gets. This is a premium callback.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana searching for the cable remote in Ilana's apartment — finds a dictaphone; Ilana: 'That's my old dictaphone from high school.'
jokes.setup: They're searching for the remote; Abbi pulls out an unidentified object
jokes.punchline: Ilana had a dictaphone in high school — which is an unusual and funny thing for a teenager to have, implying early-onset Ilana-ness
jokes.explanation: Setup for the dick-phone joke; mildly funny on its own as a character detail.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Is a dick phone?' / Ilana: 'Dic-ta-phone.' / Abbi: 'Oh, I was... "Hello? Hello, Dick."'
jokes.setup: Abbi misreads 'dictaphone' as 'dick phone'
jokes.punchline: Abbi immediately performs a bit where she pantomimes calling a man named Dick on the phone, as if that's the natural conclusion
jokes.explanation: Abbi's 'Hello, Dick' improv is the better half of the joke — she sidesteps the expected 'it's a phone for dicks' pun in favor of the name Dick, which is slightly more interesting. Character-appropriate: Abbi being almost but not quite as raunchy as Ilana.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Great idea number nine. It's like Facebook, but just for photos. I guess it could be videos too, but mostly photos.' / Abbi: 'Ilana, you invented the Instagram.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is reviewing old dictaphone recordings of her 'great ideas'
jokes.punchline: She invented Instagram on tape and apparently did nothing with it — revealed deadpan by Abbi as if this happens regularly
jokes.explanation: Strong joke — the idea that Ilana had a genuinely billion-dollar idea and filed it away as 'number nine' in a high school dictaphone and never acted on it is perfect character comedy. Abbi's 'That's insane' delivery as a second punchline is well-timed. The dictaphone setup pays off immediately.

jokes.text: Ilana's drug logic: 'I bet I was high the last time I had my remote. Maybe if I get high again I'll remember where I put it.' / Abbi: 'Aren't you already high?' / Ilana: 'I mean, like, more high. Is "higher." "Highest."'
jokes.setup: Ilana realizes she was probably high when she lost the remote
jokes.punchline: Her solution is to get higher — already being high is not a disqualifier, just an insufficient starting point. The grammar correction ('Is "higher." "Highest."') suggests she's already impaired mid-explanation.
jokes.explanation: The joke has two layers: first, the logic of getting higher to find what you lost while high (internally consistent but objectively broken), and second, Ilana's mid-sentence grammar self-correction showing she's already not fully functional. 'Certainly, yes' before Abbi's question is a beautiful detail — she preemptively agrees with the accusation. High character integration; this is unmistakably Ilana logic.

jokes.text: Ilana's weed-induced memory flashback sequence — Brooklyn apartment → living room → couch → 'Sex on the couch' → 'Bad sex. Really bad sex. The worst sex. Dale!'
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to remember where she last had the remote by retracing her stoned memories
jokes.punchline: The memory trail leads to Dale — the escalation from 'sex' to 'bad sex' to 'THE worst sex' to just 'DALE!' (as a category of badness) is the joke
jokes.explanation: The structure is cinematic — slow memory reconstruction → then the verbal escalation perfectly captures how remembering something bad works: each step gets worse until you hit the person's name like a wall. 'Dale!' as a one-word condemnation that contains all the context is excellent writing. The 'Eww... Dale.' button is the perfect coda. Also sets up the Dale plot.

jokes.text: Abbi's Craigslist speculation: 'That dude just posted for women, and then just kills them. Or do you think he has sex with them and then kills them? Or did he kill them, then have sex with them?' / Abbi's Craigslist date: 'You're both right.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and her friend are discussing the Craigslist Killer before a Craigslist apartment viewing
jokes.punchline: 'You're both right' — the man who posted the Craigslist ad is standing RIGHT THERE and has heard the entire conversation, and he confirms the murder-then-sex sequence
jokes.explanation: Outstanding structure. The debate about kill-then-sex vs sex-then-kill is dark but has the absurd internal logic of a genuine ethical dilemma. Then the intercom activates MID-CONVERSATION and the stranger confirms both possibilities with 'You're both right.' The timing is perfect: they've been overheard, the man is confirming the worst-case scenario, and his tone is that of someone confirming a coffee order. 'Please enter and come on up' is bonus horror. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: The Craigslist room-viewer Mark: 'Oh, sorry, this is Mark. Please, enter me. I mean, please, enter me. Please enter and come on up.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and her friend ring the buzzer at a Craigslist apartment
jokes.punchline: Mark says 'please enter me' twice — both times the same Freudian slip — before finally correcting to 'please enter and come on up,' suggesting this is a recurring issue for him
jokes.explanation: The double Freudian slip is a classic comedy move — saying it twice makes it less of a mistake and more of a pattern. The correction 'please enter and come on up' still contains a double entendre, suggesting Mark can't escape it. Solid if not exceptional.

jokes.text: Ilana calls Dale; before she can ask her question, Dale says 'Herpes? Yeah, all over.' — preemptively confessing
jokes.setup: Ilana is calling Dale to ask about her cable remote — she says 'this is kind of an awkward conversation to have, But do you have...'
jokes.punchline: Dale jumps to 'Herpes? Yeah, all over.' — interpreting the awkward setup as an STI inquiry and immediately confessing before she can finish
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection setup: 'awkward conversation' primes the audience and Ilana both — but we're thinking remote, not herpes. Dale's instant 'Herpes? Yeah, all over' is a triple hit: he interprets the setup correctly (relative to his life), he's been sitting on this confession, and 'all over' is the perfect escalating detail. Then Ilana's 'What? I have herpes?' pivot — she accepts his confirmation as universal — is a great second beat. The back-and-forth 'Yeah, me neither. I thought you were someone else' is perfect. One of the best-structured jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Dale's romantic suggestions: 'Why don't you come over? I'll make my encrusted salmon.' / 'What about that Italian restaurant where our souls merged?'
jokes.setup: Ilana just needs the remote back; Dale is treating the call as a romantic overture
jokes.punchline: 'Encrusted salmon' and 'where our souls merged' are both deeply Dale — the salmon sounds vaguely unappetizing ('encrusted' rather than 'crusted'), and 'souls merged' is grandiose language for what Ilana will confirm was a casual and terrible arrangement
jokes.explanation: The 'encrusted salmon' is a small gem — 'encrusted' sounds slightly wrong, like a man who's trying but doesn't quite have the culinary vocabulary. 'Where our souls merged' is excellent romantic overreach for what will turn out to be a train-schedule booty call.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Our souls never merged, dude.' / Dale: 'Yeah, I know.' — the saddest two words in the episode
jokes.setup: Dale invokes the Italian restaurant 'where our souls merged'
jokes.punchline: 'Yeah, I know' — Dale knew. He just said it anyway. The gap between what he hoped and what he knows is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Three words that are both funny and slightly devastating. Dale is self-aware enough to know 'souls merged' was never true — he's trying it anyway because hope. The joke is in the word 'Yeah' — complete agreement, zero recalibration. It's a classic deadpan-understatement moment where the comedy and the pathos occupy the same space.

jokes.text: Mark: 'It's so deep, you could wash a baby in there.' / [pause] 'I have.'
jokes.setup: Mark is proudly showing off the deep sink
jokes.punchline: He has, in fact, washed a baby in the sink — confirmed flatly, with no elaboration. Whose baby? Why? We will not find out.
jokes.explanation: Perfect beat structure: setup a hypothetical → immediately confirm the hypothetical → provide no context. 'I have.' is the ideal two-word punchline — all the comedy is in the gap between the friendly sales pitch and the unexplained baby-washing history. The pause before 'I have.' is where the laugh lives. Economy score near-maximum. Strong 8+.

jokes.text: Mark: 'So what's your shoe size?' / Abbi: 'Nine.' / Mark: 'Hmm. Men's or women's?'
jokes.setup: Mark is showing the apartment; the shoe size question is completely unexpected
jokes.punchline: 'Men's or women's?' — the follow-up makes the question even weirder and more threatening. It's a detail that has no innocent explanation.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in the escalation of the shoe size question — it starts odd and gets immediately odder. 'Men's or women's?' turns a weird question into a sinister one. Perfect economy, and it lands on Abbi's decision to leave, making the question retroactively the last straw.

jokes.text: Ilana finds another room in the Craigslist apartment: 'Abbi, check it out — there's another room in here!' Mark: 'I see you found my breakfast nook.'
jokes.setup: The apartment has revealed itself as increasingly large and odd
jokes.punchline: Mark names the room a 'breakfast nook' — a word that's domestically benign — but the shoe size question preceding it makes everything sound sinister. Ilana's enthusiasm vs. Abbi's growing panic is the comic tension.
jokes.explanation: Sustained cringe — 'breakfast nook' is domestically normal but contextually alarming. Ilana's 'cool-ass, big-ass nook' is peak Ilana enthusiasm applied to the wrong situation.

jokes.text: Mark: 'The one bathroom is in there. But it's pretty much your private bathroom. I almost never go. Except when I do go, I really go. It's a major problem. You'll have to stay in a hotel for a couple days. I'll pay for it, of course.'
jokes.setup: Mark pitches the private bathroom as a perk
jokes.punchline: The perk comes with a massive caveat — his bathroom episodes require the tenant to evacuate the premises. Then 'I'll pay for it, of course' lands as if this is a reasonable and generous offer
jokes.explanation: Escalation structure works perfectly: private bathroom (good) → he almost never goes (fine) → when he does, it's severe (alarming) → you'll need a hotel (catastrophic) → I'll pay for it (generosity framed as consideration). 'Of course' is the key word — Mark genuinely thinks this is a reasonable arrangement. The bathroom becomes the show-stopper of what was almost a perfect apartment.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'What hotel?' / Mark: 'Holiday Inn Express?' / Ilana: 'She'll take it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is clearly leaving; the hotel question is rhetorical
jokes.punchline: Ilana, unfazed by everything, accepts the Holiday Inn Express deal on Abbi's behalf — the hotel name adds a perfect layer of downmarket specificity
jokes.explanation: Ilana's willingness to negotiate on Abbi's behalf in real-time — during an active exit — is very character-true. 'She'll take it' as a fait accompli while Abbi is trying to leave is excellent. 'Holiday Inn Express' is the right specific brand: not a Marriott, not a Motel 6, exactly the middle ground of crisis accommodation.

jokes.text: Dale park meeting: Ilana brings 'It's a boy' balloons — 'It's all they had left at Key Foods.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is meeting her ex at a park; she brings something — we assume flowers, coffee, etc.
jokes.punchline: She brings 'It's a boy' balloons because nothing else was available — she shows up to a tense ex-encounter with blue baby balloons and explains herself with complete calm
jokes.explanation: The visual of Ilana arriving to an emotionally charged ex-encounter holding 'It's a boy' balloons is excellent physical-context comedy. 'It's all they had left at Key Foods' is the perfect excuse — she considered bringing something, she just didn't prioritize appropriateness. Very Ilana.

jokes.text: Dale: 'You don't have to pretend anymore. I know why you left that remote. You want to get back together. And I'm in.' / Ilana: 'Dale, we were never together!'
jokes.setup: Dale has constructed an elaborate romantic narrative from Ilana leaving a cable remote at his apartment
jokes.punchline: 'We were never together!' — the entire romantic mythology Dale has built is demolished in four words. The gap between his interpretation and reality is maximal.
jokes.explanation: The premise that leaving a remote constitutes a romantic signal is perfectly delusional. 'We were never together!' as a response to 'you want to get back together' is a great escalating denial — you can't break up from what didn't exist. Dale's certainty vs. Ilana's flat correction is the ideal comedy pairing.

jokes.text: Ilana defines the relationship: 'We were roommates who had sex a small handful of times because the train wasn't running to Manhattan on the weekends.'
jokes.setup: Dale believes they were in a relationship
jokes.punchline: 'Because the train wasn't running to Manhattan on the weekends' — the relationship was purely logistics. The MTA schedule was the only force keeping them together.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best line. 'A small handful of times because the train wasn't running to Manhattan on the weekends' is extraordinary writing: it precisely quantifies the relationship ('small handful'), explains its sole motivation (transit infrastructure failure), and contains the entire emotional universe of a certain kind of New York situationship. Every word is load-bearing. 'The train wasn't running to Manhattan on the weekends' as the foundational reason for sexual activity is both hilariously specific and completely recognizable as NYC logic. Deeply character-integrated: only Ilana could say this without shame, and only Ilana would have been in this arrangement. Comparable to the Snip Snap anchor in terms of quotability and specificity.

jokes.text: Dale: 'Say the word, and I'll leave my fiancée.' / Ilana: 'You're engaged?' / Dale: 'And you brought her?' — she is apparently present
jokes.setup: Dale is declaring he'd leave everything for Ilana
jokes.punchline: He brought his fiancée to this secret romantic meeting in the park — the fiancée is right there
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Dale is engaged AND brought the fiancée to his romantic ambush is a strong escalation. The fiancée's presence makes this both more chaotic and more cruel. Ilana's 'And you brought her?' has perfect comic indignation — she's less upset about the engagement than about the logistical insanity of bringing her.

jokes.text: Dale: 'Just say you'll give us a shot, and I'll give the driver a signal, and he will take her straight to Newark airport.'
jokes.setup: Dale wants to leave his fiancée for Ilana
jokes.punchline: He has a pre-arranged plan: a driver, a signal, and a specific destination (Newark). This was all prepared in advance — Dale arrived at this park meeting with a getaway car for his fiancée.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Newark airport' is the key — not JFK, not LaGuardia. Newark. It suggests a one-way ticket to somewhere Amanda can start over, chosen for budget reasons or to maximize inconvenience. The pre-arranged signal/driver reveals Dale has been planning this heist for a while. The elaborate logistical preparation for a romantic betrayal executed in a public park is both hilarious and horrifying.

jokes.text: Dale's exiting disclosures: 'Please don't tell Amanda I told you I'd leave her. Please don't tell her I bought her a one-way ticket to Santa Fe. What? Please don't tell her I have herpes!'
jokes.setup: Dale is leaving after failing to win Ilana back
jokes.punchline: Each 'please don't tell' is worse than the last — the Santa Fe ticket (not Newark anymore?), the one-way confirmation, and then the herpes callback landing as the final disclosure
jokes.explanation: Triple-threat escalation structure: each revelation is exponentially worse. Newark changed to Santa Fe (suggesting he has multiple tickets?), the herpes callback is perfectly placed as the final sin. The escalation pace — three quick punches — is well-timed. The herpes callback brings the joke full circle from the phone call and lands with more force for the wait.

jokes.text: Ilana to herself: 'Do I or do I not have herpes? I do, you don't. Oh, God.'
jokes.setup: Dale's parting disclosure about herpes has reopened Ilana's uncertainty
jokes.punchline: Ilana talks herself through it — 'I do, you don't' — and then is horrified all over again by the 'I do' part
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of 'I do, you don't' working it out in real time is very Ilana — she talks through problems as if narrating to a friend who isn't there. 'Oh, God.' as the response to her own 'I do' is a well-structured self-delivered punchline.

jokes.text: The perfect roommate apartment: crown molding, built-ins, quiet sex, beach weekends — and Abbi settles it with 'Yeah, so is mine, with myself, right?'
jokes.setup: The potential roommates say their 'sex is on the quiet side'
jokes.punchline: Abbi pivots to her own sex life — with herself — equating solo sex with couple sex and laughing awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Abbi's nervous oversharing about her sex life (with herself) to potential roommates is relatable and character-true — she's the one who says slightly too much in an effort to connect. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: The SelectDate commercial turns out to be a racist dating site: 'For whites who are single and ready to mingle... the final solution to your dating problems.'
jokes.setup: The $8K illustration Abbi sold to a 'sexy-ass new dating website' is now on TV; everyone watches proudly
jokes.punchline: 'For whites who are single and ready to mingle' + 'the final solution' — Abbi's wholesome illustration is revealed to be the face of a white nationalist dating service with Nazi-coded language
jokes.explanation: The episode's most audacious joke and its structural climax. The entire 'Started from the Bottom' premise — the $8K as Abbi's big break — collapses into horror. 'The final solution to your dating problems' uses Nazi language as a tag for a dating site, which is both offensive-on-purpose and a perfect satirical skewering of how racists use euphemisms. The callback structure is flawless: we've been celebrating this money for the whole episode, and this is the payoff. Abbi's mortified breakdown is also a great reaction beat.

jokes.text: Abbi, panicked and alone with the shocked potential roommates: 'I love all races. "Races," not "racists." You guys are— I wouldn't— And it's a— It's probably not national. It's just like, um— Probably it's just—'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just watched her illustration anchor a white nationalist dating ad in front of people she wants to live with
jokes.punchline: Her damage control spirals — 'races not racists' is a self-correction that makes things worse, and 'probably not national' is the most optimistic possible spin on a TV commercial
jokes.explanation: '"Races," not "racists"' is a perfect example of damage control that makes it worse — pointing out the distinction draws more attention to the problem. 'Probably not national' as the optimism floor is excellent. Abbi's verbal flailing is character-specific: she's trying very hard and digging deeper with each word.

jokes.text: Ilana's awkward explanation to the potential roommates: 'I'm so sorry. I don't know her that well. I'm just bonin' her friend, and so I just hang out with her. You know how you gotta hang out with your girl's friends?'
jokes.setup: Ilana tries to help Abbi by distancing herself
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'help' involves (1) claiming not to know Abbi well, (2) reducing their friendship to proximity from a sex arrangement, and (3) asking the shocked potential roommates to relate
jokes.explanation: 'I'm just bonin' her friend' as an alibi is a masterpiece of unhelpful help — it throws Abbi under the bus while also making Ilana sound terrible, and then appeals to the bystanders' sense of social obligation with 'You know how you gotta.' The entire line is Ilana trying to save a situation by making it worse in a completely different direction. Deeply character-integrated.

jokes.text: The potential roommates' reaction: a sustained, horrified silence — 'Hmm.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just explained that she's only there because she's 'bonin' Abbi's friend
jokes.punchline: The one-syllable 'Hmm' is all the response either Abbi or Ilana's explanation merits — it contains volumes of judgment
jokes.explanation: The 'Hmm' beat is perfect comedic economy — one syllable concluding a chaotic sequence. Maximum judgment per syllable. In context, it's the sound of the perfect apartment slipping away.

jokes.text: The cable company reveals Ilana returned the wrong remote — they hold up visual proof: 'This is the wrong remote. This is the right remote.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has finally come in person, having found a Dale-remote, to resolve the cable situation
jokes.punchline: It's the wrong remote — she went through the herpes scare, the Dale encounter, and the park meeting to retrieve the wrong remote
jokes.explanation: The wrong-remote reveal is structurally satisfying as a Sisyphean payoff — all that effort for nothing. The episode needed this beat to honor the absurdity of the cable subplot.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I had to find out I may or may not have herpes to get this remote!' — summarizing her day to the cable company
jokes.setup: Ilana is being told the remote is wrong
jokes.punchline: She catalogs her trauma — herpes uncertainty is the headline grievance — to an employee who has no context and cannot help
jokes.explanation: 'May or may not have herpes' acknowledges the ambiguity that the episode carefully maintained — Ilana doesn't know for certain, and that uncertainty becomes the most memorable part of her day. Delivered to a stranger who doesn't care is the classic Ilana move.

jokes.text: Cable company employee reveals they are the only employee: 'I also do billing, installation programming, and I compose the on hold music.' Then: 'Oh no, no, this is the "waiting room" music. I compose that too. I'm actually the only person who works here.'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks to speak to a manager
jokes.punchline: There is no manager — the single employee does everything, including composing both the hold music AND the waiting room music as distinct categories
jokes.explanation: The joke escalates by division: manager → also does billing → also installation → also composes music → wait, TWO musics (hold music AND waiting room music are different) → only person. The distinction between hold music and waiting room music is a perfect absurdist detail — the effort to subcategorize is both sad and funny. Explains the entire cable nightmare: a one-person company running on bureaucratic chaos.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Oh, and you know what? I hate your music.' Employee: 'So do I.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is furious about the nickels situation
jokes.punchline: She attacks the hold music; the employee — who composed it — agrees that they hate it too. The creator hates their own creation and is trapped listening to it alone all day.
jokes.explanation: Four words — 'So do I.' — deliver extraordinary character pathos and comedy simultaneously. The employee has been so isolated in this one-person cable company that they've developed contempt for their own creative output. The implied daily existence — alone, listening to music they hate, serving almost no customers — is a tiny portrait of workplace despair that's both funny and unexpectedly moving. Economy score of 9.5 is justified by the density of implication in three syllables.

jokes.text: The resolution: 'You can either mail in a check for $1,000, or come in person and pay $5.' / 'You're only the fifth person ever to come in.'
jokes.setup: Ilana wants to resolve the cable charge
jokes.punchline: $1,000 vs. $5 — the discrepancy is enormous, and the fact that only four people before Ilana ever chose the $5 option suggests most people never come in person and are probably still paying forever
jokes.explanation: The $1,000 vs $5 split satirizes cable company absurdity perfectly — there's no logical reason for the price discrepancy except institutional contempt. 'You're only the fifth person ever to come in' is the devastating detail: this business model has been running on the assumption that no one will bother.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I can pay in quarters, right?' / Cable employee: 'Oh, actually no. Our system's down and we only take nickels. Sorry.'
jokes.setup: Ilana offers quarters — which she has, from the bank's nickel-to-quarter exchange at the beginning
jokes.punchline: The cable company only takes nickels — the exact denomination Ilana started with and then converted. She's come full circle and is now on the wrong end of the exchange.
jokes.explanation: One of the best structural jokes in the episode. The nickel/quarter setup was planted at minute 2 and pays off at minute 17 — Ilana went to the bank specifically to convert nickels to quarters, and the only place that would have been useful is the cable company, which requires nickels. The absurdity compounds perfectly: a working system was undone by a bureaucratic requirement for a specific coin denomination. The structure is near-flawless. This is rewatch-bonus comedy — it rewards viewers who remembered the bank scene.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Oh, and you know what? I hate your music.' / Cable employee: 'So do I.'
jokes.setup: Ilana vents at the cable employee as a parting shot
jokes.punchline: The composer of the music agrees — 'So do I.' — delivered with complete resignation, as if this is something he's thought about every single day alone in this office
jokes.explanation: Three-word punchline that earns its weight. The sole employee composing music he hates for a company he's the only member of, suffering his own creation forever — it's a perfectly constructed character tragedy played for a laugh. 'So do I.' has the rhythm and resignation of the best deadpan delivery.

jokes.text: Bevers attempts to apologize with bagel bites arranged in the shape of an 'A' — 'because I know how you like to label your food'
jokes.setup: Bevers wants to apologize for masturbating in the living room
jokes.punchline: His apology gift is food arranged in her initial — the specificity of 'I know you like to label your food' reveals he pays close attention to Abbi's habits while doing nothing useful with this knowledge
jokes.explanation: The bagel bites 'A' is the right apology gift for Bevers — low-effort but oddly thoughtful in the wrong direction. 'Because I know how you like to label your food' is the killer detail: he has observed her food-labeling habits but not registered that masturbating in her living room is unacceptable. Character-specific and economical.

jokes.text: Bevers's extended masturbation description — 'I like to start off slow... work the tempo up and up... lick a thumb... brush it lightly against the nipples... there's a [bleep] there... I like to apply pressure... then right when I'm [bleep], I [bleep], then I [bleep] into the [bleep], hard, hard, hard. And then I [bleep], and it just— it— it— It's a very powerful feeling.'
jokes.setup: Bevers wants to explain/apologize for what Abbi walked in on; she repeatedly tells him to stop
jokes.punchline: He describes his masturbation technique in clinical, escalating detail despite constant protest — the censored bleeps create a rhythm that suggests something very specific while remaining technically unknown; 'It's a very powerful feeling' is the transcendent button
jokes.explanation: Long-form cringe comedy executed with precision. The joke escalates through several distinct beats: the instructional tone (tempo, thumb-lick), the anatomical specificity ('there's a [bleep] there'), the escalating rhythm ('hard, hard, hard'), and the philosophical conclusion ('a very powerful feeling'). Bevers performing apology-as-tutorial is perfectly character-integrated — he believes explanation constitutes closure. The bleeps create their own comedy: they're heavily used in a way that implies an extremely specific act while leaving it unknowable. The 'Whoa, cool shirt' button (joke 76) is what makes this a scene rather than just a bit.

jokes.text: After Bevers's entire masturbation description, he ends with: 'Whoa, cool shirt.'
jokes.setup: Bevers has just delivered a minute-long masturbation tutorial despite protests
jokes.punchline: 'Whoa, cool shirt.' — Bevers interrupts himself (or concludes) by noticing Abbi's shirt, pivoting from graphic self-description to casual compliment with zero transition
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. After a sustained, escalating, uncomfortable masturbation description over Abbi's protests — a sequence that builds and builds — the punchline is four words about a shirt. The structural misdirection is genius: the audience is braced for either a climax to the description or Abbi's explosion, and instead gets a clothing compliment delivered with casual sincerity. 'Whoa' is the perfect word — it's genuine surprise, suggesting Bevers noticed the shirt mid-sentence and just had to say something. The tonal whiplash is maximized by what preceded it. This is comedy architecture at its best — a minute-long setup for a four-word button that could not be less about masturbation. Near the 9-10 anchor range.

jokes.text: Child at the store who lectures Ilana: 'I'm seven, and you're being inappropriate.' / Ilana: 'Bitch, you're 22.'
jokes.setup: A child overhears Ilana's herpes announcement and calls her out
jokes.punchline: Ilana refuses to be corrected by a child — counter-claims the child is 22 — suggesting Ilana cannot be disciplined by anyone she perceives as a peer
jokes.explanation: 'Bitch, you're 22' is a perfect Ilana line — she processes the child's authority challenge by reassigning their age to a peer bracket, which both eliminates the child's authority and reveals Ilana doesn't know how to be scolded. 'Bitch' applied to a seven-year-old is the transgression that makes it funny and quotable. Very high character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana admits to Lincoln: 'your dentist and I have been having sexual intercourse on the regular for about five months' — Lincoln: 'Ilana, it's been a year and a half!'
jokes.setup: Ilana is confessing her ongoing arrangement with Lincoln to contextualize the herpes discussion
jokes.punchline: She's off by a year — she thinks five months, it's been 18 months — which means she has no reliable memory of their entire relationship
jokes.explanation: The humor here isn't just that she's wrong — it's that she's wrong by 13 months, in the context of an episode about lost memory and drugs. 'Sexual intercourse on the regular' is a formal phrase that sounds like it's being read into a legal record. Lincoln's incredulous '18 months' vs. Ilana's five-month estimate is a gap that characterizes their whole dynamic.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Don't do drugs. That's— That's the takeaway. Don't do drugs. It's like I have no memories, man. I can't remember a thing! I wake up, I'm like aah! It's scary.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just realized she's missing 13 months of memories
jokes.punchline: 'Don't do drugs' — delivered as a genuine PSA with no apparent awareness that she's currently high and will continue doing drugs; the advice is sincere and completely unheeded
jokes.explanation: The anti-drug PSA from someone who is demonstrably still using drugs, delivered with no irony intended, is classic Ilana. 'I wake up, I'm like aah!' captures the chaos without self-awareness. The punchline 'Don't do drugs' is funnier for being genuinely meant.

jokes.text: Ilana never found the right remote: 'But wherever it is, I hope it's happy.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln asks if she ever found the right remote — the plot's entire MacGuffin
jokes.punchline: Ilana wishes the remote well — extends empathy to an inanimate object — and the cable saga ends with no resolution and sincere concern for the remote's emotional state
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode-closing joke. The entire cable remote subplot — nine months of charges, a Dale encounter, a herpes scare, a wrong remote, a nickel/quarter reversal — ends with Ilana expressing hope that the remote is in a good place emotionally. 'I hope it's happy' is the most Ilana possible conclusion: detached from the practical problem, attached to an imagined interior life of an object. Maximum character integration, near-maximum economy. Strong 8+ — it closes a major plotline with the exact right note.
